caveats: a: The occupation source covers all instrument manufacturing at NAICS 334500.
a: Engineering occupations contain both AI-exposed information work and non-substitutable accountable design decisions.
rho: No public five-year AI implementation study specific to 334511 was located.
rho: Controlled-data environments can prevent use of general-purpose cloud AI.
e: The frozen firm count is margin-bridged rather than observed in the EBITDA band.
e: A change of control can require customer, licensing, security, or export-control actions.
s5: Gallup covers all employer businesses rather than aerospace and defense instrument manufacturers.
s5: Strategic acquisitions may not qualify under the intended operator lens.
q: Commercial avionics and defense sole-source programs have different capture economics.
q: Cost-type and fixed-price contracts transmit productivity benefits differently.
d5: The BLS projection is four-digit and ten-year.
d5: Backlog and global market forecasts are not equivalent to constant-quality U.S. demand.
o: Some functions can migrate into integrated multifunction hardware or software-defined systems.
o: Production may be outsourced while the qualified design authority remains accountable.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Revenue arises from product deliveries, production releases, development and engineering contracts, upgrades, spares, repair, overhaul, service, and lifecycle support. Programs are often platform-specific, qualification-heavy, and sold to prime contractors, government agencies, aircraft or vessel OEMs, operators, and allied customers under fixed-price, cost-type, commercial, or negotiated purchase arrangements.
lens.deviation_from_default: The code combines defense and commercial, airborne and marine, component and integrated-system businesses. The lens retains independent manufacturers with repeat qualified external-customer programs and transferable production, engineering, and compliance operations; captive prime-contractor units and pure development businesses are excluded to maintain a coherent control-acquisition population.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive divisions of primes or OEMs, GPS equipment classified elsewhere, aircraft engine instruments, meteorological equipment, pure software or consulting firms without manufactured systems, pre-revenue technology developers, shells, and non-control investments.
lens.included_activities: Independent lower-middle-market manufacturers repeatedly supplying qualified search, detection, radar, sonar, navigation, guidance, flight-instrument, mission-computer, avionics, and related sensing or control systems to external defense, aerospace, marine, government, and commercial OEM customers.

a.bridge: BLS measures occupations for four-digit NAICS 334500 rather than six-digit 334511. The 2023 mix includes 26.89% production, 17.58% architecture and engineering, 12.47% management, and 7.77% office and administrative support, alongside computer, business, scientific, sales, and service occupations. The range applies partial task exposure to systems engineering, proposal and cost support, documentation, software assistance, configuration management, planning, procurement, test-data analysis, quality records, and administration while excluding most physical assembly, integration, calibration, field service, and accountable safety or mission decisions. The parent proxy may understate engineering intensity in complex avionics and radar firms but overstate it for production-heavy instrument suppliers.
caveats: The occupation source covers all instrument manufacturing at NAICS 334500.
Engineering occupations contain both AI-exposed information work and non-substitutable accountable design decisions.
a.change_evidence: A 334511 wage-weighted task inventory by commercial, defense, airborne, and marine segment, with current automation and clearance status, would materially change the range.
a.rationale: Complex sensing and guidance systems produce large engineering, requirements, configuration, test, maintenance, proposal, and program datasets that AI can assist. Classified or controlled information, physical integration, rigorous test, certification, and mission accountability preserve substantial human work.

d5.bridge: BLS projects real output for four-digit NAICS 334500 from $149.7 billion in 2024 to $184.7 billion in 2034, a 2.1% annual rate; compounding five years gives approximately 1.11. The range is widened because 334511 is more exposed than the parent category to defense budgets, radar and electronic warfare modernization, commercial aircraft production and retrofit, marine systems, program timing, and geopolitical demand. Mercury Systems cites third-party estimates of aerospace and defense electronics growth from $157 billion in 2025 to $197 billion in 2029, but that global Tier 2 market is broader than U.S. 334511 and likely overstates some commercial or legacy niches.
caveats: The BLS projection is four-digit and ten-year.
Backlog and global market forecasts are not equivalent to constant-quality U.S. demand.
d5.change_evidence: Inflation-adjusted 334511 shipments, funded backlog, program-of-record budgets, aircraft and vessel production, retrofit installation rates, spares and service demand, export licenses, bookings, and cancellations would materially change the estimate.
d5.rationale: Defense modernization, contested sensing, electronic warfare, autonomy, aging-aircraft retrofits, commercial aviation, space, maritime awareness, and lifecycle support sustain demand. Program cancellations, budget timing, procurement delays, platform concentration, export restrictions, supply shortages, and commercial aviation cycles create downside.

caveats: The frozen firm count is margin-bridged rather than observed in the EBITDA band.
A change of control can require customer, licensing, security, or export-control actions.
e.change_evidence: A 334511 enterprise census linked to ownership, EBITDA, contract backlog, program and customer concentration, security clearances, DDTC registration, certifications, IP rights, management depth, and change-of-control clauses would materially change the estimate.
e.rationale: An eligible target must be independent, correctly sized, externally recurring, compliant, transferable, and supported by durable programs. Many establishments are divisions of large defense or aerospace groups, pre-revenue developers, single-program subcontractors, or firms whose clearances, export registrations, intellectual property, certifications, customer approvals, or key engineering talent may not transfer cleanly.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 334511 ($16,111,340,630) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 334511 ($52,297,168,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.2532 (margins.json[334]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Computers/Peripherals EBITDA/Sales 25.32%); revenue/employee $414,774 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 334511); class <5: 105 firms, avg revenue/firm $851,676 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $215,644 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 47 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,133,170 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $540,119 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 49 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,812,184 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $965,245 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 97 firms, avg revenue/firm $12,401,753 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,140,124 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 31 firms, avg revenue/firm $45,188,806 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $11,441,806 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 44 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,120,837,795 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $283,796,130 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 97

caveats: Some functions can migrate into integrated multifunction hardware or software-defined systems.
Production may be outsourced while the qualified design authority remains accountable.
o.change_evidence: Evidence of widespread customer self-manufacture, software-only substitution for physical sensors and instruments, or fully virtual supply without an accountable integrator would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: Search, navigation, radar, sonar, guidance, and avionics systems require an accountable operator to design, integrate, manufacture, qualify, secure, document, test, support, and deliver physical mission equipment. AI can become part of the system and automate internal work, but safety, defense, export, and customer accountability keep an operating manufacturer or integrator necessary.

caveats: Commercial avionics and defense sole-source programs have different capture economics.
Cost-type and fixed-price contracts transmit productivity benefits differently.
q.change_evidence: Contract and subcontract terms showing pricing basis, allowable-cost treatment, change clauses, annual negotiations, spares margins, sole-source status, service renewals, customer sharing, and margin retention following process improvement would materially change the estimate.
q.rationale: Design-in status, mission qualification, proprietary IP, certification, program longevity, spares, service, and the cost and risk of switching suppliers can preserve productivity benefits. Capture is constrained by powerful prime contractors and governments, competitive procurements, certified cost or pricing data, price-reasonableness review, fixed-price risk, negotiated productivity expectations, and platform concentration.

caveats: No public five-year AI implementation study specific to 334511 was located.
Controlled-data environments can prevent use of general-purpose cloud AI.
rho.change_evidence: Audited deployments among 334511 suppliers reporting engineering and proposal hours, test cycles, defects, rework, schedule adherence, downtime, security accreditation effort, qualification outcomes, and implementation cost would materially change the estimate.
rho.rationale: AI can support requirements traceability, document search and drafting, proposal preparation, software development, configuration control, planning, procurement, predictive maintenance, and test-data triage. Implementation is slowed by classified and export-controlled data, cybersecurity, customer-approved systems, FAA or military qualification, AS9100-style quality controls, low-volume programs, validation, and the need for cleared accountable reviewers.

s5.bridge: Gallup reports that 52.3% of U.S. employer businesses are owned by people age 55 or older and 22% of employer-business owners planned a sale or transfer within five years. The estimate discounts this cross-industry intention rate for strategic-prime acquisitions, internal succession, failed diligence, customer or program consent, clearance and export-control issues, intellectual-property limits, and nontransferable founder engineering. The proxy can understate active consolidation in defense electronics but overstates completed qualifying LMM control transfers.
caveats: Gallup covers all employer businesses rather than aerospace and defense instrument manufacturers.
Strategic acquisitions may not qualify under the intended operator lens.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of 334511 closed transactions, buyer type, owner ages, broker listings, foreign-ownership review, DDTC change notices, failed deals, asset sales, and closures would materially change the estimate.
s5.rationale: Aging ownership and strategic demand for niche defense and avionics technologies support transfers, while national-security regulation, program concentration, controlled technology, and qualification continuity narrow the set accessible to a general LMM control buyer.

principal_driver: Long-lived qualified programs and rising sensing, radar, electronic warfare, navigation, and retrofit needs can support durable demand and defensible supplier relationships.
principal_weakness: The accessible target pool is narrowed by captive ownership, strategic competition, controlled technology, government pricing oversight, security requirements, and single-program concentration.

run_meta.title: Search, Detection, Navigation, Guidance, Aeronautical, and Nautical System and Instrument Manufacturing
